speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] amazing, it's amazing. What we've got now, you get a little reflection, but not bad. You do live video mostly? Actually, I do mostly-- I do more video work now, but for most of my career, I've been a film director photographer. Film. Commercials. Music videos. I just started doing video in the last couple years, and I never worked with this camera until I started this project. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape one. My name is Scott Fried, S-C-O-T-T F-R-I-E-D, and I'm here on March 26, 1995 with Dr. Eric Nash in Forest Hills, New York, United States of America. And we're conducting this interview in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh. Can I say something now, or is it too late?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Good morning, Dr. Nash. Can you please, first, spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eric J. Nash, E-R-I-C. Middle initial J. Last name, N-A-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that your same name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a C in between the S and the H when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth and location?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on July 28th, 1910 in a little town in Czechoslovakia called Holesov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-L-E-S-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. I'd like to begin by talking a little about your family in prewar Czechoslovakia. Can you tell us a little bit about your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in this little town as a good Jewish family. We were not very religious, but we were definitely Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived my first eight years in this little town. I went to a public school there, which was a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was only Jewish children. It was not religious as a Jewish school, but it was sequestered from the other kids of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that required by law?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a school that the Jewish community had set up. And that's where I was enrolled. I went through the first three years of schooling there. And by then, it was World War I, and we were rudely awakened out of our peaceful life by a pogrom that took place in Holesov after World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can you remember before that any experiences during World War I? You were a child, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a very little child. I remember that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were very patriotic, and my father was very prosperous at that time. He was a grain merchant. And he was very much for our emperor. That was Franz Joseph I, and later on, Karl. And of course, we followed the events of World War I, as far as I can remember, very closely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was life for-- for Jews in the-- I guess you were in the Austria-Hungarian empire?

speaker: interviewee
text: The life was not bad at all. However, there was always a creeping anti-Semitism all over middle Europe, and particularly Eastern Europe. We had-- as far as I remember-- no bad experiences, as far as this is concerned, until the end of the war, when there was a short period without law and order. There was no government established yet in the new Czech Republic. And there was a real murderous pogrom which cost us our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I very vividly remember that there was-- I was a little over-- over seven years old. There was shooting and-- and yelling. And my father grabbed me out of the bed in the middle of the night, and we went up to the attic of our house to hide from these hordes that invaded every Jewish house. As we later learned, two Jews were shot during the-- and killed during the pogrom. And it was quite horrifying, because we heard them plundering the place and robbing everything that was in sight. And then they were talking about putting the house on fire, which of course would have been our end. But then, they thought better of it, or they were chased away. I don't know. And after many hours of anguish, we went back to our place to find it in shambles. There was not a drawer that wasn't emptied out, and the whole place was just ruined. So as we were in our night gowns and with a winter coat over it, my father took me to the railroad, and we went to Vienna. It happened to be that my mother and my other siblings were in Vienna at that time. And later on, horrified by these experiences, we moved to Vienna. So for my eighth year on until 1936, we lived in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't live in Czechoslovakia when it became a new country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there at the birth, but-- and I was in and out with my father. But we were not there when the state was established.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did it feel like though when you found out that your area was becoming a new country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a child, and I didn't have too much feelings about it, but I can only tell you that later on, when I was in and out of Czechoslovakia, and later, when I served in the Czech army, I realized that Czechoslovakia was as close to a economic, cultural, and a social paradise as you can want to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no economic crisis as it was all the time in Austria, where we lived. There was a prosperous country. Everybody did well. There was no-- hardly any unemployment. And life there was just beautiful. As far as we Jews is concerned, the first president, Tomas G. Masaryk, went through a long period of self re-education. And out of the inborn anti-Semitism grew a man who was very, very tolerant. And the life of the Jews in Czechoslovakia was as free as you can want it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet your parents decided to stay in Vienna though throughout the 20's and 30's.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father had established a business in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- as a grain and later lumber merchant. And we settled in Vienna. We went to school there. My brother went to the university. He became a lawyer. And my sister was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was school like for you in Vienna as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, I entered public school in the third grade. And when I entered the class, I was approached immediately by a guy, and he says, are you a Jew? That was the first question I got, so there was, in Vienna, of course, a subdued but always present anti-Semitism. And-- but we took it in stride, because it wasn't really malignant. It wasn't violent or bad. Of course, you-- you were ostracized because you were a Jew. We were, in my class, only three or four.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was an integrated school as opposed to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they were integrated. This was a normal public school. And because of the surroundings, my father took me out of this school and put me into a much better public school in the city. And there, there was absolutely no anti-Semitism that I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your community that you lived in, in Vienna was that a Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were members of the Jewish community called the Kultusgemeinde. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were not in any kind of ghetto. Everybody lived in his apartment. And we had a beautiful apartment in the third district of Vienna, which is one of the better areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said your family was not what you'd call very religious, but did you celebrate certain holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We celebrated Friday night, and we celebrated every high holidays. And my father went to temple whenever there was a holiday and also on Saturdays. So we were raised in a somehow semi-religious background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe, perhaps, a typical Sabbath in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid there's not much to describe. We had candles. And we had a more festive meal Friday night. I don't think we kept the laws too well. I-- I-- we-- we did drive a car, and we did go on the tram. So we were not very strict, as far as the laws is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was it just you and your two parents in the household?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were three children in our household. My old-- my brother-- I was the youngest. My brother lived with us, and my sister. Later on, much later, my brother got married and moved out. But my sister married late in her life, and she lived with us, and her husband, until our deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you describe for us the political climate of Austria as you got older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was pretty stable. It was a democracy. There were the two large parties, the Social Democrats and the Christian Socialists. And the government was always Christian Socialists, as far as I remember. One of the Bundeskanzlers, which is the head of the government, was a priest with the name of Seipel. And the Catholic church wielded an enormous power. And as I said, there was no open violent anti-Semitism, but there were obstacles in our way all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such as when my brother was looking for a job, and he became a lawyer. Or on the university, there were always friction and fights between [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rightist elements. And of course, they were mainly anti-Semitic and against the Jews. There were the so-called student organizations, which were known and notorious for their anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was your family Austrian citizens or Czech citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never became Austrian citizens, although we tried to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why weren't you able to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Austrian-- it wasn't easy to become an Austrian citizen [INAUDIBLE]. And somehow, it never succeeded. As an interesting anecdote, my brother became-- was the only one who became an Austrian citizen, and that was my doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- in 1935, I worked as a physician in Carlsbad, which is Czechoslovakia. It may be confusing that I went back and forth. But by then, I had finished my medical studies, and I was about to enter the army. And I took a job in Carlsbad, which was a world renowned spa. And we had all celebrities of the world coming there. Among others, I had the governor of one of the Austrian states of Salzburg as my patient, Mr. Riehl. And Mr. Riehl became very attached to me, although I was Jewish. And privately, they always were very friendly. It was always just publicly that they had to show some anti-Semitism. And I told him the trouble that my brother is in because he's not an Austrian citizen. And within a week, he had his Austrian citizenship. So that was a-- a-- a interesting event, but we never became Austrian citizens, which was-- when I later on went to medical school, was a disadvantage. Number one, my tuition was much higher, because I was a foreigner. And number two, I was unable to get any kind of a job that paid, because I was not an Austrian. But later on, I was very happy not to be an Austrian, because I then had to do my military duties, which were two years of service. And I always was very fond of Czechoslovakia, and I was determined to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you said you became a physician. Now, where did you go to school and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to what is called in middle Europe, middle school, but here it's called high school. That's called a gymnasium. And I graduated in 1928. And the same year, my father died of a heart attack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, then, and against my family's wishes, I entered medical school. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now, why didn't they want you to enter medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father had died, and we were no longer in very good financial situations, and they wanted me to go out and work and make a living. But I promised them I would not be a burden, and I always worked all through my studies to pay my tuition myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult getting into medical school as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. There was no problem to enter medical school. Whoever wanted to enter just entered. You just inscribed yourself, and you were a medical student.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe those years in medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young, and there is nothing more beautiful than to study medicine. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you attend by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you attend school for medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the medical school of the University of Vienna. At that time, the medical school of Vienna was by far the leading medical authority in the world. Names that are now saints in the medical science were my teachers. Whether it was Sigmund Freud, or whether it was Wenckebach, or Eiselsberg, or Denk, or Kwastek. All these names are in all medical books, mentioned all the time. And at the time, of course, I didn't know how lucky I was, but they were all my teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large Jewish faculty?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no Jewish faculty. I mean, I think-- I think Sigmund Freud was the only Jew that ever got a-- a professorship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Once you were a Jew, you couldn't get further than to be a [NON-ENGLISH]. That means you had lectures, and you were teaching. But you did not get any professorship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about as a student? Were you involved in any movements at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were in constant fights with the radical right, with the student organizations they had at that time. The scars on the cheeks from the fencing. They were dueling. They had certain caps, like that showed them to be members of certain student organizations. And they were absolute rabid anti-Semites. And it came to physical fights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one, a specific incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whenever they approached us, usually five against one, using [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] funny names and assaulting us, we got into a fight. And we were, at that time-- just as an aside-- we were studying anatomy, and we were dissecting bodies at that time. And one of these fights, I vividly remember, wound up that we used corpses as weapons. We just used bones or-- or arms, extremities. As sad, as horrible as it sounds, it was very funny. Interestingly enough, our anatomy professor was a former Jew. Of course, he couldn't remain a Jew. Otherwise, he wouldn't be a professor. Professor Tundler. And he was quite leftist. He was a Social Democrat, and he always came to our help. Not physically, but he supported us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was your knowledge of the outside world? Germany? United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My medical studies were from 1929 to 1934. That means in the years where national socialism blossomed in Germany, and of course, we followed with horror and with the greatest of interest what was going on in Germany. When Hitler came to power in January of 1933, I was in the end phase of my medical studies. And it was, I remember, quite distracting, what was going on there. And we had to buckle down and study for our tests.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember specifically when he came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. I remember it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- there was no television. But there was radio. And of course, there was a newspaper. And we were very well informed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what kind of a reaction did his power take in Austria? To the Austrian people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a strong Nazi movement in Austria. I-- I want to recall to you that there was an Austrian government, which was also a fascist government. First Adolphus, who was assassinated by a Nazi in-- in his office. By the way, Mr. Panetta, who killed him, became a big celebrity after Hitler took over. And there was hardly a town that didn't have a Panetta Place, a Panetta Street. And his follower, Mr. Schuschnigg, was trying very hard to keep Austria out of German grasp. And of course, he couldn't succeed. He, in his desperation, wanted a plebiscite when things became very dangerous. And, of course, Hitler knew that he would lose the plebiscite, so he marched right in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a rise in anti-Semitism in Austria after Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Definitely. I mean, you could see people with the brown shirts, with the sleeves on the arms with a swastika. And they were organized just like in Germany. And there were the beginning of street fights with leftists. With-- we didn't have any communists in Austria, but the Social Democrats were the-- the leftists. And there were definitely signs that this was a rising power. They also had-- when later on, the Nazi party was outlawed, they found another method. They wore white stockings. And you know, they had-- whoever wore a white stocking was a Nazi. And it-- it was frightening how many there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you graduated, did you go and practice in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I graduated, I-- I did what you would call here a rotating internship. I got my postgraduate education in various Viennese hospitals. And that was for the next two years until I was called in for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any problem in these rotations that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. There was always a latent creeping anti-Semitism, although it was not in the open until Hitler came to power. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it did not really interfere with my education. We-- we could go after our business.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you went and served in the Czech army. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on October 1st of 1936, I entered the Czech army and served for two years. Being a doctor, we had a special basic training in Prague. And it was a very rough basic training. And I can easily say now that this probably is also one of the items that made me survive during the bad years to come, because we were really in-- in good-- good shape. After the basic training, I was a physician in the Czech army. I was a officer. And I had, to be honest, a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewer
text: No anti-Semitism in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no-- there was hardly any anti-Semitism. If I say hardly, among us doctors, there were a lot who didn't make any secret of it that they are anti-Semites. Nevertheless, they-- they did not talk to me. They had no social contact with me. We were-- the-- the few Jews who were in the army, we were quite popular. And-- and we didn't really feel any ostracism or-- or elimination. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life in Czechoslovakia was, as I mentioned before, was almost paradisic. I mean, it was just what-- what the doctor ordered. There was complete freedom. There were 20 political parties from-- of all denominations, and everything was booming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Czech Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a very small Czech nationalist party, negligible. The-- the-- the vast majority was either Social Democrat or Christian, mostly Catholic. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had absolute equality in front of the law or in our economic strides. There was-- there were-- you could become whatever you wanted in Czechoslovakia. Our minister of health was a Jew, for instance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as you're in the-- in the army, did you-- when did you first begin to sense a problem with Germany, being in the Czech army in the late '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was in the Czech army between '36 and '38, Hitler was in bloom. I mean, the Nuremberg laws were passed. All the [INAUDIBLE] positions had been eliminated. He was in complete control. He already had occupied the Rhineland. And we saw-- we should have seen the writing on the wall. But we felt very secure, because we had-- since World War I, a-- a whole series of military arrangements with England, with France, and with Russia. And all three of these super powers at that time were obligated to come to our help in case we should be attacked. We also had-- this was called large entente. There was a small entente consisting of Poland, Yugoslavia, Romania, and Czechoslovakia. And they too were absolutely obligated by treaty to come to our help. And it was a very terrible disappointment when we were sold down the drain at the Munich Conference. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- we were mobilized.
